title: RAPTOR: Recursive Abstractive Processing for Tree-Organized Retrieval
one_liner: Recursively cluster and summarize chunks into a multi-level summary tree, index every level in one vectorstore, and retrieve top-down so queries can match at the right level of abstraction.
problem_solved: Flat top-k chunk retrieval answers narrow factoid questions but fails on broad, thematic, or synthesis questions whose answer is distributed across many chunks or whole documents; RAPTOR adds LLM-generated cluster summaries at multiple abstraction levels so both overview and detail queries have a matching index entry.
failure_modes: Retrieval works for specific facts but fails on broad questions like 'what are the main themes / overall conclusions of this report'
The answer is scattered across many chunks or sections, and top-k retrieval only surfaces disconnected fragments
On a large document collection, retrieval either misses important details or gets swamped by irrelevant chunks
Retrieved chunks lack the document-level or section-level context needed to interpret them correctly
Questions require multi-hop or thematic reasoning that no single chunk supports
One index must serve queries at very different granularities (executive summary vs. exact figure lookup)
when_to_use: Your corpus is long-form documents (reports, books, documentation, transcripts) where facts depend on surrounding sections and users ask both overview and detail questions
Users frequently ask 'summarize', 'compare across sections', or 'main takeaways' style questions that a flat chunk index handles poorly
The corpus is relatively static, so the expensive one-time tree build is amortized over many queries
You want traceability from high-level answers back to source chunks (the tree stores parent/child and origin metadata per node)
You already use hierarchical two-level indexing (doc summaries + chunks) and need more abstraction levels
tradeoffs: Expensive index build: one LLM summarization call per cluster per level (plus re-embedding each level), so cost and build time scale with corpus size and tree depth
Corpus updates require rebuilding (or carefully patching) the tree — poor fit for frequently changing document sets
Summaries can lose or distort fine details; answers grounded in summary nodes are one step removed from verbatim source text
Index is larger: the vectorstore holds original chunks plus every summary at every level

Extra hyperparameters and moving parts: cluster count (notebook uses a fixed heuristic n_clusters = min(10, n_texts // 2) rather than the paper's UMAP + GMM with BIC selection), max_levels, per-level summarization prompts
This notebook's online path adds latency: hierarchical retrieval loops over levels and contextual compression makes an LLM extraction call per retrieved document

Implementation caveat: the notebook's hierarchical_retrieval narrows to children by appending 'AND id:(...)' text to the embedded query string, which is not a real metadata filter — a production port should filter on child_ids properly (the tree_traversal_retrieval variant does use FAISS callable metadata filters)
Overkill for small corpora or purely factoid QA where plain top-k similarity search already works
how_it_works: Offline (indexing, the dominant stage): load a PDF with PyPDFLoader and take page texts as level-0 nodes. build_raptor_tree then loops up to max_levels: embed current texts with OpenAIEmbeddings, cluster the embeddings with a scikit-learn GaussianMixture (n_clusters = min(10, n_texts // 2)), and for each cluster call summarize_texts — a 'Summarize the following text concisely' ChatPromptTemplate piped to ChatOpenAI(gpt-4o-mini). Each summary becomes a node at the next level with metadata {level, origin, child_ids, id}, preserving parent-child links; the loop stops early when only one summary remains. build_vectorstore flattens ALL levels (original chunks + every summary) into LangChain Documents and builds a single FAISS index. Online (retrieval + post-retrieval): hierarchical_retrieval starts at the top level and walks down, retrieving per level and following child_ids; an alternative tree_traversal_retrieval uses similarity_search_by_vector_with_relevance_scores with callable metadata filters on level/id. Retrieved nodes pass through a ContextualCompressionRetriever whose LLMChainExtractor keeps only query-relevant spans, and raptor_query concatenates the compressed docs into context for a final LLMChain answer, returning the answer plus per-document level/origin details for traceability.
key_dependencies: langchain (FAISS vectorstore wrapper, ChatPromptTemplate, LLMChain, ContextualCompressionRetriever, LLMChainExtractor, Document)
scikit-learn (GaussianMixture clustering, PCA for cluster visualization)
numpy / pandas (per-level DataFrames of text, embedding, cluster, metadata)
repo helper_functions / evaluation.evalute_rag (PyPDFLoader and eval utilities)
key_code_signals: GaussianMixture(n_components=n_clusters, random_state=42).fit_predict(embeddings) — GMM clustering of embeddings at each tree level
build_raptor_tree(texts, max_levels=3) — recursive embed -> cluster -> summarize loop producing per-level DataFrames with {level, origin, child_ids, id} metadata
summarize_texts(): ChatPromptTemplate('Summarize the following text concisely') | ChatOpenAI(model_name='gpt-4o-mini')
build_vectorstore(): FAISS.from_documents over Documents from ALL tree levels (originals + summaries) in one index
tree_traversal_retrieval(): similarity_search_by_vector_with_relevance_scores with lambda metadata filters on level and parent ids, recursing down via child_ids
hierarchical_retrieval(): loop from max_level down to 0, extending results and following child_ids
raptor_query(): LLMChain over concatenated compressed context for final answer, returning per-doc level/origin details
related_techniques: hierarchical-indices: refines — RAPTOR generalizes two-level (document summary + chunk) indexing into a recursive multi-level summary tree
contextual-compression: composes — the notebook wraps the tree vectorstore in a ContextualCompressionRetriever with LLMChainExtractor to trim retrieved nodes
graph-rag: alternative — graph/community-summary architecture targeting the same global, thematic question failure mode
microsoft-graphrag: alternative — entity/community-based hierarchical summarization instead of embedding-cluster summarization
semantic-chunking: complements — semantically coherent base chunks improve level-0 cluster quality before tree building
context-enrichment-window-around-chunk: alternative — cheaper fix when the problem is only local context around a retrieved chunk, not corpus-wide synthesis
reranking: complements — could reorder mixed-level retrieved nodes before generation
summary: RAPTOR attacks the failure where flat chunk retrieval answers detail questions but fails on broad, thematic, or cross-section questions whose answer is spread across many chunks. At index time it recursively builds a summary tree: embed all chunks (OpenAIEmbeddings), cluster them with a Gaussian Mixture Model (scikit-learn), summarize each cluster with an LLM (gpt-4o-mini via LangChain), then repeat on the summaries for up to max_levels until a single root summary remains. Every node — original chunks and all summaries — is stored with level and parent/child metadata in one FAISS vectorstore, so a query can match at the appropriate abstraction level. Retrieval starts at the top level and walks down through child_ids (hierarchical or tree-traversal retrieval), and retrieved nodes pass through a ContextualCompressionRetriever (LLMChainExtractor) before final answer generation. Costs: O(clusters x levels) LLM summarization calls at build time, a larger index, full rebuilds on corpus changes, and possible detail loss in summaries. Prefer it for long reports and 'main themes' queries over static corpora; skip it for small corpora or purely factoid lookup.
notes_for_chunking: Structure is clean and function-per-cell. Cell 0 is a book advertisement (strip). Cell 1 is a long, fully self-contained overview markdown cell with its own ## subsections (Overview, Motivation, Key Components, Method Details, Benefits, Conclusion) — chunk it by subsection; it is the only substantive prose in the notebook. Cell 2 is an image reference (strip or keep as pointer). Cells 3-5 are pip install and git clone boilerplate (strip). Cell 6 is the imports block (useful as dependency signal, low retrieval value). From there the notebook alternates one short ### header markdown cell ('Helper Functions', 'RAPTOR Core Function', 'Vectorstore Function', 'Define tree traversal retrieval', 'Create Retriever', 'Define hierarchical retrieval', 'RAPTOR Query Process (Online Process)') with exactly one code cell defining 1-4 functions — headers are terse labels, so keep header + code cell together as one chunk; the code carries all the content. Code-to-markdown ratio is high after cell 1. Example-usage cells at the end are short one-liners (tree build, vectorstore, query). No large outputs or base64 blobs in this copy; final cell is a tracking-pixel markdown image (strip).
